narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your application included a solid LSAT score of 38, which is above the typical student's score of around 37, along with plans to attend full-time like most applicants, a lower-middle family income background, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.6. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3 and Year 3 ranking of 1 were notably lower than the averages of about 6 for both. The admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 58.9%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes students likely to succeed on that critical exam. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which stood out as a strong signal of potential challenges ahead compared to typical students who rank much higher. Your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction back toward passing more than any other positive factor, showing it was a highlight in your profile. Your Year 1 decile also nudged the odds toward failure, though less intensely than Year 3, placing you below the norm for admitted students. Smaller positives like your family income and full-time status helped a bit, while your undergraduate GPA had a minor negative tilt. Overall, your academic decile rankings, especially in Year 3, outweighed your strengths when compared to the profiles of students who typically pass the bar. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or mentorship tailored for Black women in law, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas—many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort. We're rooting for you.
